title: AP U.S. History SAQ Practice Exam 05

questions.prompt:
Source A: Original secondary interpretation written for this practice exam, not an actual quotation.
A historian of Jacksonian politics argues that the 1820s and 1830s marked a major expansion of democratic participation for many white men. According to this interpretation, the decline of property qualifications, party organization, campaign rallies, and higher voter turnout made politics more open and competitive than it had been in the early republic.
Source B: Original secondary interpretation written for this practice exam, not an actual quotation.
A historian of democracy and exclusion argues that Jacksonian politics expanded participation for some Americans while reinforcing the political exclusion of others. According to this interpretation, the same period that celebrated the “common man” also restricted the rights of women, African Americans, and Native peoples and used state power to support removal and racial hierarchy.
(a) Describe one major difference between the two interpretations of Jacksonian democracy.
(b) Explain one piece of historical evidence that supports the argument in Source A.
(c) Explain one piece of historical evidence that supports the argument in Source B.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Describes a valid difference, such as Source A emphasizing expanded political participation among white men, while Source B emphasizes exclusion, racial hierarchy, gender limits, or coercive policies affecting Native peoples and African Americans.
(b) 1 point: Explains relevant evidence supporting Source A, such as expanded white male suffrage, the rise of mass political parties, nominating conventions, campaign rallies, increased voter turnout, the Democratic Party, the Whig Party, or popular campaigning.
(c) 1 point: Explains relevant evidence supporting Source B, such as women’s exclusion from voting, African American disfranchisement in many states, Indian Removal, the Trail of Tears, limits on free Black rights, slavery’s expansion, or racialized definitions of citizenship and political belonging.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should treat Jacksonian democracy as both expansionary and exclusionary. It should explain who gained greater political participation and who remained excluded or harmed by the same political culture.

questions.prompt:
Source: Original simulated source based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
A freedwoman writing to a Freedmen’s Bureau official, South Carolina, 1866.
“My husband and I have agreed to work this year’s crop, but the planter says our children must labor in the field instead of attending the school opened near the church. We know the meaning of a contract if both sides keep it. We ask that wages be written, that families not be separated, and that our children have time to learn letters. Freedom should mean more than choosing whose cotton we pick.”
(a) Identify one historical development that shaped the situation described in the source.
(b) Explain how the author’s point of view likely influenced the argument made in the source.
(c) Explain one way the source reflects broader struggles over freedom and labor during Reconstruction.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a relevant development, such as emancipation, Reconstruction, the creation of the Freedmen’s Bureau, the transition from slavery to free labor, Black Codes, labor contracts, sharecropping, or African American efforts to build schools and families after slavery.
(b) 1 point: Explains that the author’s perspective as a freedwoman and worker shaped her emphasis on family autonomy, written wages, children’s education, protection from coercion, and a broader meaning of freedom beyond plantation labor.
(c) 1 point: Explains a broader struggle, such as conflict between freedpeople and former enslavers over labor discipline, the meaning of free labor, access to education, family independence, landownership, legal rights, or the limits of federal protection in the South.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should emphasize African American agency during Reconstruction. It should connect labor contracts, education, and family autonomy to freedpeople’s efforts to define freedom after emancipation.

questions.prompt:
Source: Described data chart created for this practice exam.
The chart is titled “Energy, Oil Imports, and Public Policy in the United States, 1960–1980.”
Indicator | 1960 | 1973 | 1980
Share of U.S. petroleum consumption supplied by imports | about 18% | about 35% | about 40%
Average gasoline price | relatively low | rising sharply | much higher
Federal attention to fuel efficiency and conservation | limited | increasing | significant
Public concern about dependence on foreign oil | moderate | high after oil embargo | very high
Common visible effects | highway growth and suburban commuting | gasoline lines in some regions | smaller cars, conservation campaigns, energy policy debates
The chart notes that figures are approximate and intended to show broad historical trends.
(a) Describe one change in the United States reflected in the chart.
(b) Explain one international or domestic factor that contributed to the change described in part (a).
(c) Explain one political, economic, or cultural consequence of the energy changes reflected in the chart.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Describes a valid change, such as rising dependence on imported oil, higher gasoline prices, increased federal attention to energy conservation, public concern about foreign oil, or changes in consumer behavior.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant factor, such as the 1973 oil embargo, OPEC, Middle East conflict, suburbanization and automobile dependence, declining relative domestic oil production, inflation, environmental concerns, or increased energy consumption after World War II.
(c) 1 point: Explains a relevant consequence, such as energy policy debates, fuel-efficiency standards, conservation campaigns, smaller cars, economic strain, inflationary pressure, environmental regulation, debates over nuclear power, or public questioning of U.S. dependence on foreign oil.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should connect energy use to both foreign policy and domestic life. It should avoid treating the energy crisis as only a temporary gasoline shortage and should explain how it shaped politics, consumer habits, environmental debates, and economic concerns.
